title: The Smell of Mummies Tells Ancient Stories
summary: Scientists have discovered that the old smell of Egyptian mummies can teach us about how people were preserved long ago. Mummies are bodies wrapped in cloth from ancient Egypt, and they have a special musty scent. Researchers at the University of Bristol found that this smell comes from chemicals used during mummification, not just from decay. By studying tiny pieces of mummies, they identified smells from fats, oils, beeswax, plant resins, and bitumen. These chemicals show that mummification methods changed over 2,000 years, becoming more advanced with expensive materials. The study helps us learn about Egyptian history and how they cared for the dead.

background_read: Ancient Egyptians lived in Egypt a long time ago and practiced mummification to preserve bodies after death. They wrapped bodies in cloth and used special substances to keep them from decaying. This process was part of their beliefs about the afterlife. Scientists today study mummies to understand history and how people lived in the past. The smell of mummies is a new way to learn about the materials and methods used, helping us uncover secrets from thousands of years ago.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from the University of Bristol
WHAT: Studied the smell of Egyptian mummies to learn about mummification
WHERE: In ancient Egypt and in a laboratory
WHY: To understand how preservation methods changed over 2,000 years
